coredata.dc:title: Thiomersal photo-degradation with visible light mediated by graphene quantum dots: Indirect quantification using optical multipath mercury cold-vapor absorption spectrophotometry
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part B: Atomic Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Miranda-Andrades, Jarol R.
Khan, Sarzamin
Toloza, Carlos A.T.
Romani, Eric C.
Freire Júnior, Fernando L.
Aucelio, Ricardo Q.
coredata.dc:description: Thiomersal is employed as preservative in vaccines, cosmetic and pharmaceutical products due to its capacity to inhibit bacterial growth. Thiomersal contains 49.55% of mercury in its composition and its highly toxic ethylmercury degradation product has been linked to neurological disorders. The photo-degradation of thiomersal has been achieved by visible light using graphene quantum dots as catalysts. The generated mercury cold vapor (using adjusted experimental conditions) was detected by multipath atomic absorption spectrometry allowing the quantification of thiomersal at values as low as 20ngL−1 even in complex samples as aqueous effluents of pharmaceutical industry and urine. A kinetic study (pseudo-first order with k=0.11min−1) and insights on the photo-degradation process are presented.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiomersal
Graphene quantum dots
Photo-degradation
Mercury
Cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of graphene quantum dots
Analytical procedure for determination of Hg by multipass-CV-AAS
Analytical procedure for determination of ethylmercury by GC-CV-AFS
Preparation solutions, standard solutions and working solutions
Study of the conditions to improve efficiency of the visible radiation photo-degradation of thiomersal mediated by GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of thiomersal (20mgL−1) with detail showing the effect of radiation exposure (a. 0min, b. 15min, c. 30min, d. 60min, e. 80min) to A) visible light and B) UV. C) Absorption spectra of thiomersal (20mgL−1) in the presence of GQDs: (a) without incidence of UV and (b) after 50min irradiation with UV, with detail showing the effect of UV radiation exposure; D) Hg measured signal from the UV treated thiomersal solution (50μL aliquot) in the presence of GQDs along UV incidence (error bar is the standard deviation for n =3).
Scanning transmission electron microscopic image of: A) GQDs nanoparticle dispersion after 50min visible radiation exposure; B) GQDs nanoparticle dispersion after 50min UV radiation exposure.

A) Absorption spectra of thiomersal (20mgL−1) in the presence of GQDs under the exposure to visible light: (a) 0min, (b) 15min, (c) 30min, (d) 60min, (e) 80min. B) Hg measured signal from the UV treated thiomersal solution (50μL aliquot) in the presence of GQDs (after dilution) along visible radiation incidence (error bar is the standard deviation for n =3).

A) Hg measured signal (using CV-AAS after fast reduction with SnCl2) from solutions of thiomersal containing different volumes of the synthesis dispersion of GQDs; B) Time profile of the measured Hg release during visible radiation exposure of thiomersal (20mgL−1) in the presence of different volumes of the synthesis dispersion of GQDs: a) 50μL, b) 20μL, c) 17μL and d) 17μL with H2O2 (1%).

Hg measured from the photo-degraded thiomersal in the presence of 17μL aqueous dispersion of GQDs: A) in function of pH (25min irradiation time); B) in function of time at pH5.0; C) after 25min irradiation time in the presence of either formic acid or hydrogen peroxide; D) in function of time at pH5.0 in the presence of hydrogen peroxide 2% v/v. Error bars are the standard deviations for n =3.

A) Chromatogram (GC-CV-AAS) showing the formation of HgEt+ of a thiomersal solution along irradiation time: a) 0min, b) 5min, c) 10min, d) 15min, e) 20min. B) Chromatogram (HPLC with detection at 222nm) showing thiomersal and degradation products peak along time: a) 0min, b) 5min, c) 10min, d) 15min. Thiomersal solution containing 17μL of dispersion of GQDs, 2% H2O2 and at pH5.

Hg measured signal by multipath-CV-AAS: A) in function of the concentration of thiomersal. B) and C) in function of irradiation time of a thiomersal solution with C0 is the initial concentration (20mgL−1) and with Ct as concentration measured at different irradiation times. D) Effect of EtHg+ on the thiomersal signal measured as Hg. Thiomersal solution containing 17μL of dispersion of GQDs, 2% H2O2 and at pH5. Error bars are the standard deviations for n =3.
Information on methods for the determination of thiomersal reported in the literature and the proposed method.

row.entry: RP-HPLC indirect fluorimetric detection and UV derivatization

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: RP-HPLC-UV: reversed-phase high-perform liquid chromatography with absorption detection in the UV; FIA: flow-injection analysis; MW: microwave; CV: cold vapor technique; AAS: atomic absorption spectrometry; AFS: atomic fluorescence detection.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Selected paper from the 14th Rio Symposium on Atomic Spectrometry. Vitória, Brazil, 2 April – 7 April 2017.

head.ce:title: Thiomersal photo-degradation with visible light mediated by graphene quantum dots: Indirect quantification using optical multipath mercury cold-vapor absorption spectrophotometry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro 22451-900, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Swabi, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Anbar 23561, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro 22451-900, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pontifical Catholic University of Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiomersal is employed as preservative in vaccines, cosmetic and pharmaceutical products due to its capacity to inhibit bacterial growth. Thiomersal contains 49.55% of mercury in its composition and its highly toxic ethylmercury degradation product has been linked to neurological disorders. The photo-degradation of thiomersal has been achieved by visible light using graphene quantum dots as catalysts. The generated mercury cold vapor (using adjusted experimental conditions) was detected by multipath atomic absorption spectrometry allowing the quantification of thiomersal at values as low as 20ngL−1 even in complex samples as aqueous effluents of pharmaceutical industry and urine. A kinetic study (pseudo-first order with k=0.11min−1) and insights on the photo-degradation process are presented.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiomersal (thimerosal) is commonly employed as preservative in cosmetic products, pharmaceutical formulations and vaccines [1,2] as it inhibits bacteriological growth. Thiomersal contains 49.55% of mercury in its composition and its degradation produces thiosalicylic (TSA) acid and dithiobenzoic acid (DTBA) besides ethylmercury (EtHg+) that contaminates consumers and patients [3,4]. The use of thiomersal in vaccines and pharmaceuticals is controversial as ethylmercury is highly toxic and it may cause severe neurological disorders [5,6].

The extensive use of thiomersal also raises environmental concerns because of the disposal of expired vaccines and medicines, also due to mercury contaminated wastewater from pharmaceutical industries. The allowed limit of mercury in discharged aqueous effluents is 5μgL−1 which is equivalent to 10μgL−1 of thiomersal [7].

Reverse-phase high-performance liquid chromatography (RP-HPLC) with absorption photometric detection has been used to determine thiomersal [3,8–13] in ophthalmic solutions and vaccines with detected amounts of thiomersal as low as 0,025μg (50μL of introduced sample) [8]. Alternatively, thiomersal can be derivatized with dithizone before introduced into the HPLC system [11]. RP-HPLC coupled with inductively coupled plasma (ICP) mass spectrometry has also been used to determine thiomersal, through the 202Hg isotope, in vaccines and in their diluent solutions [12]. Authors were able to monitor the expected levels of thiomersal in samples (about 100μgmL−1) evaluating the degree of preservative degradation along shelf time. Pillar da Silva et al. have implemented electrochemical determination of thiomersal in ophthalmic solutions by RP-HPLC [3] with best results achieved by ion suppression (using tetraethylammonium perchlorate as counter-ion) and coulometric detection that enabled 6μgL−1 limit of detection (LOD). Acosta et al. [13] used HPLC coupled to a spectrofluorometer to determine thiomersal after it was converted (in an on-line UV reactor) to the fluorescent thiosalycilic acid (315/442nm). LOD of 729μgL−1 was achieved with application to the analysis of aqueous effluents and river water.

A colorimetric method was proposed based on the complex formation between thiomersal and dithizone in basic medium (maximum absorbance at 538nm) [14]. The method was applied to monitor thiomersal in viral hepatitis vaccines allowing the quantification of absolute quantities as low as 200μgL−1. The spectrophotometric determination of thiomersal in ophthalmic formulations was achieved (at mgL−1 level) by using partial least squares and hybrid linear analysis of their original spectrophotometric absorption spectra [15]. Fredj et al. used a flow injection analysis system producing elemental mercury from thiomersal [16], which vapors were separated in a gas diffusion cell and diffused through a Teflon membrane into a permanganate solution stream where Hg was oxidized to Hg2+, decreasing the permanganate absorbance at 528nm. The method was applied for vaccine samples with LOD of 70μgL−1. Differential pulse voltammetry enabled determination of concentrations as low as 0.81μgL−1 after pre-concentrating thiomersal on a mercury-silver film electrode [17].

As thiomersal can be converted to elemental mercury, atomic spectrometric determination of mercury can be used to indirectly determine thiomersal at very low quantities. Gil et al. photo-decomposed thiomersal using UV in a solution containing H2O2 [18]. The further reduction of Hg2+ to Hg was achieved in a second step by ultrasound after addition of formic acid. The mercury cold vapor (CV) was carried to the quartz cell of an atomic absorption spectrometer to be detected at levels down to 40μgL−1. Dos Santos et al. converted thiomersal into Hg, in solution containing formic acid, using UV [19]. The mercury CV formed inside the coiled reactor was carried out to the ICP optical emission spectrometry (OES) system to be determined. LOD was 300μgL−1 of Hg (600μgL−1 of thiomersal). He et al. photo-decomposed thiomersal using UV and formic acid further detecting the Hg in a dielectric-barrier discharge micro-plasma-OES system (LOD for thiomersal of 0.17μgL−1) [1]. By the direct discreet sampling of a vaccine solution into the direct-barrier discharge micro-plasma, Wu et al. [20] produced mercury CV that was carried to an atomic fluorescence (AF) spectrometer (LOD of 0.06μgL−1). As very low LOD values for Hg can be achieved by AF spectrometry, many works have reported to indirectly determine thiomersal. A flow injection system proposed by Campanela et al. comprised a reactor where thiomersal was converted to Hg2+ by the assistance of microwaves and UV [21]. Reduction was made in another on-line reactor using NaBH4 with the formed Hg directed to the Ar/H2 flame of the AF spectrometer to be measured (LOD of 3μgL−1). In another work [22], thiomersal (along with ethylmercury and Hg2+) were determined after separation in an RP-HPLC system that was coupled to an AF spectrometer. On-line post-column mercury generation was achieved with UV with the aid of formic acid (LOD for thiomersal was 0.09μgL−1). Recently, Yepsen et al. have used heterogeneous photo-catalysis (using TiO2 dispersed in acidic solution and UV) to produce elemental Hg from thiomersal that was determined by CV-AF spectrometry [23]. In Table 1 quantification limits for thiomersal achieved by different analytical methods are summarized.

Quantum dots (QDs) are nanometric size semiconductor materials that present narrow bandgap between the valence and the conductance discreet energy levels produced due to quantum confinement. Depending upon their size, electronic transitions are achieved upon incidence of visible light. Also due to their small size, QDs can be easily stabilized in aqueous solutions, forming a nanoparticle dispersions with very large surface area to interact with other substances in solution, for instance to allow photo-degradation processes to occur, as it was achieved by using CdS QDs (surface modified with polyvinyl chloride) [24] to catalyze photo-degradation of pollutants such as dyes and phenols by the produced electrons, holes and free radicals. The so called graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanometric fragments of graphene sheets, as a monolayer, few-layer or multilayer (carbon dots when in several layers), where electron transport is confined in their dimensions. Contrary to QDs of Cd, GQDs are carbon-based structures that are bio-compatible and present low toxicity. GQDs presents a wide absorption band that covers part of the visible spectrum. Recently, the catalytic effect of GQDs on the photo-degradation of fuchsin dye, using visible light, was achieved by Roushani et al., after 100min because of both a photocatalytic process and a photosensitization process as the dye also absorbs in the visible range [25]. Other works have reported the interaction between the negatively charged GQDs surface and Hg2+ affecting the nanomaterial excitonic recombination [26,27].

In this study, a new method was developed for the indirect quantification of thiomersal after an easy and efficient photo-degradation assisted by GQDs. The photo-oxidation assisted by QDs converted thiomersal into ethylmercury then to Hg2+, using visible radiation, to further be chemically converted into Hg in a chemical reduction cell coupled to a dedicated mercury portable atomic absorption spectrometer with an optical multipath cell. In this way, a very sensitive indirect determination of thiomersal was accomplished with the analysis of effluents of pharmaceuticals industry pharmaceutical formulations and urine samples.

Mercury measurements were made on a dedicated mercury CV multipass atomic absorption spectrometer (multipass-CV-AAS), model RA-915 (LUMEX, Russia), equipped with Zeeman background correction and connected to a RP-92 accessory for chemical reduction in aqueous solution. An automated analytical system (GC-CV-AFS) for mercury speciation (MERX, USA), consisted of a gas chromatography (GC) system coupled with CV atomic fluorescence (AF) spectrometry (MERX, USA), was used for Hg speciation studies.

UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a Perkin-Elmer, model Lambda 35, double beam spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, UK) using 1000nmmin−1 scan rate, 10nm spectral bandpass and 1cm optical path length quartz cuvettes. HPLC study was made on a model 1200 series system (Agilent, USA) with a diode array molecular absorption photometric detector. Degassing of the solvents that constituted the mobile phase was made (off-line) in a 9L ultrasonic bath, model NSC2800 (Unique, Brazil).

The laboratory made photochemical reactor consisted of six commercial fluorescent lamps (6W each, emitting in the visible spectral range) set on the internal wall of a PVC tube (20cm diameter×30cm width) fixed onto a wood box, as a base that contain a mechanical rotor that kept the test-tube circular rack rotating (6rotationsmin−1). A small fan was placed at the top of the reactor in order to keep its internal temperature below 30°C. When necessary, the lamp set may be replaced by bacteriological fluorescent lamps (main line at 253nm and secondary lines in the 296 to 313nm range). Samples were accommodated inside 20mL glass tubes, which were placed (vertically) inside the reactor in a circular test-tube rack. A centrifuge (Sorvall Biofuge Stratos model, Thermo Scientific, USA) was used for sample treatment. Total carbon measurements were made on a Carbon Analyzer model TOC-VCPN (Shimadzu, Japan). For the pH measurements a pH-meter model MPA 210 Tecnopon (Brazil) with a glass membrane electrode combined with a reference Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) was employed.

Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) was obtained from the Milli-Q gradient A10 ultra-purifier (Milipore, USA). Thiomersal was from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The derivatization agent sodium tetra-n-propyl borate (NaBPr4) was from abcr GmbH Co (Germany). Tin chloride (SnCl2) was from Vetec (Brazil). Mercury speciation adequate acetate buffer solution (pH4.9), potassium hydroxide solution (2% v/v), standard solutions of methyl mercury (MeHgCl) at 1000mgL−1 and ethyl mercury (EtHgCl) at 1000mgL−1 were from Brooks Rand Instruments (USA). Mercuric nitrate, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, phosphoric acid, methanol (HPLC grade), hydrogen peroxide (30% vol), formic acid and citric acid were from Merck (Germany). Syringe filters (0.45μm) were from Whatman, UK. Dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight of 3.5kDa) were from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. (USA). Pharmaceutical solution (Tecnoquímicas S.A.) contained 0.1g of thiomerosal per 100mL. Nitrogen (99.99%) and argon (99.999%) were from Lynde gases (Brazil).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized based on the procedure described in the literature [28], using 0.50g of citric acid placed into a 5mL beaker that was heated at 240°C. As the mixture became molten (changing from colorless to brown in about 5min) the hot liquid was added into 50mL of NaOH 0.25molL−1 aqueous solution at room-temperature, in order to obtain (after filtering on a 0.45μm syringe filter) a clear pale yellow aqueous sample that was further dialyzed (24h). This was called the aqueous synthesis dispersion of GQDs.

Characterization of the synthesis dispersion of GQDs (after diluting it in ultrapure water) is reported in Toloza et al. [29]. Briefly, the diluted GQDs dispersion produced a wide extinction spectrum with a maximum peak at about 200nm and first excitonic band at 370nm and covering the violet-blue spectral range. The dispersion showed bright emission (maximum at 460nm) under excitation at 370nm. The quantum yield of the GQDs was about 8% at 25°C. Characteristic Raman features of graphene (bands G and D) appeared respectively near 1586cm−1 and 1358cm−1. The produced GQDs had a size distribution in the range from 3 to 8nm, with an average diameter of 5.2nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure for determination of Hg by multipass-CV-AAS
ce:section.ce:para: Sample solutions (200μL) were transferred to glass test tubes containing 10.00mL of a H2O2 (1%) solution with the pH adjusted to 4.5 (by the addition of 50 to 80μL of a 0.1molL−1 HCl solution) also containing 17μL of the synthesis dispersion of GQDs. Glass test tubes (made of quartz for some specific tests) were placed in a circular rack inside the photo-reactor and they were kept rotating in order to be uniformly exposed to the visible radiation from all of the six lamps inside the reactor. Then, the test tube content was transferred to a glass reaction cell containing 2mL of a SnCl2 aqueous solution (20% v/v) in order to provide a rapid reduction to Hg, that was transferred, by an air flow passing though the solution, to the multipass-CV-AAS system operating in a continuous acquisition mode. The next analysis was performed after removing the previous solution (though the bottom of the cell), washing with ultrapure water and SnCl2 solution until Hg signal reached baseline (this procedure took about 2min to be performed).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure for determination of ethylmercury by GC-CV-AFS
ce:section.ce:para: The GC-CV-AFS system comprised a purge module and a retention/pre-concentration module, which functions are respectively to purge the volatile derivatized mercury species from the sample into the system where they are retained in pre-concentration traps to be later desorbed. A chromatographic module and a pyrolysis module performed respectively the separations of the mercury derivatized species and the thermic decomposition of these species to Hg0 before it reaches the mercury dedicated fluorescence detection system. An aliquot (50μL) of a thiomersal solution, previously submitted to the visible radiation induced photo-degradation mediated by GQDs, was transferred to a sample flask (amber with 40mL volume and septum cap) containing 20mL of ultrapure water, then mixed with 200μL of the acetate buffer solution and 100μL of the derivatization reagent solution (1% v/v). After 15min, EtHg+ and Hg2+ were respectively converted in the volatile EtHgPr (ethylpropylmercury) and (Pr)2Hg. The flask was placed in the automatic sampler of the GC-CV-AAS system to be transferred to a purge vessel, forcing the liberation of volatile Hg species that were carried, by a argon flow (312mLmin−1) to a dried trap where they were adsorbed and then thermically desorbed to be carried, by an argon flow (34mLmin−1), through the packed chromatographic column. After separation, the Hg species zones passed through a tube section heated by a resistance to thermally reduce them to the Hg vapor detected by AF. The signal was acquired as peak height and retention times for EtHg+ and Hg2+ were respectively about 5min and 8min.

Elution was made at a constant flow rate of 1.0mLmin−1 using a pH2.5 mobile phase that consisted of a methanol/phosphoric acid solution (0.02molL−1) in a 60/40 v/v proportion. Separation was made on a 4.6×250mm Eclipse Plus C18 (Agilent, USA) with 5μm particle size kept at 25°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation solutions, standard solutions and working solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Thiomersal (sodium salt) standard stock solution (20mgL−1) were prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of standard in ultrapure water. Its aqueous intermediary solution (1000ngL−1) was prepared by diluting the stock solution in ultrapure water. Working solutions (100, 200, 300, 400ngL−1) of thiomersal were prepared by the dilution of the intermediary solution. The MeHg+ and EtHg+ intermediary solutions (at 1000ngL−1) were prepared by the direct dilution of a 1mgL−1 standard using ultrapure water. Working solutions (10 and 100ngL−1) were prepared by the direct dilution of the intermediary solution. The derivatization reagent solution (1% v/v) was prepared by dissolving 50mg of NaBPr4 in 5mL of a KOH (1% v/v) aqueous solution.

The SnCl2 (2% v/v) solution was prepared by dissolving 20g of the salt in 100mL of concentrated HCl. This solution was heated in order to evaporate half of its volume aiming the elimination of mercury contamination found in the HCl solution and avoiding contamination of the system. After cooling the solution, the ultrapure water was added to complete volume to 100mL. Before use, the solution was placed on a glass bottle washer to be purged with nitrogen gas for 15min.

For the determination of thiomersal in a pharmaceutical formulation (Merthiolate topical solution), the sample was diluted 1000 times before placing 200μL into a test tube containing 10mL of a H2O2 solution (1%) with the pH adjusted to 4.5 and containing 17μL of the synthesis dispersion of GQDs. This mixture was placed inside the reactor and irradiated for 20min. Then the total volume was transferred to the chemical reduction cell (containing 2mL of the SnCl2 20% v/v solution) connected to the multipass-CV-AAS system.

The aqueous effluents were acquired from a local pharmaceutical industry. The sample was fortified with thiomersal followed by two times dilution. Then mixture was vortex-mixed for 5min and passed through a 0.45μm filter. The solution (10mL) containing H2O2 solution (1%) with the pH adjusted to 5.0 and containing 17μL of the synthesis dispersion of GQDs were then placed into the photochemical reactor for 20min.

Fresh urine samples, donated by a volunteer, aliquots (2mL) were fortified with thiomersal, then mixed with 1.3mL of an acetonitrile/methanol 50/50 v/v solution. The mixture was vortex-mixed for 5min and then centrifuged for 15min at 500 RFC, in order to separate the protein content and other solids [30]. After this clean-up procedure, supernatant was filtered through a 0.45μm syringe filter and brought to a final volume of 10mL with ultrapure water. The solution was transferred to test tube and placed into the photochemical reactor for 20min. Then the solution was transferred to the reduction cell connected to the multipass-CV-AAS system.

Aqueous solution of thiomersal (20mgL−1), placed into glass test tubes, was firstly exposed to visible radiation in the photochemical reactor. Periodically (every 10min) an aliquot was collected, diluted and monitored by molecular absorption spectrophotometry. No changes in the typical absorption spectrum (Fig. 1A) was found (intensities measured at 205 and 233nm) over 140min experiment. Residual mercury concentration (about 10ngL−1), detected at the original solution, did not increased over the UV irradiation exposure time as thiomersal does not react with the reducing agent (SnCl2) thus no additional Hg2+ was formed to produce the Hg that was quantified by multipath-CV-AAS. In contrast, when the thiomersal solution (placed in quartz test tubes) was exposed to UV radiation, the degradation of thiomersal was observed over time (Fig. 1B) but even after 80min of UV exposure, a fraction of thiomersal (about 15 to 20%) could be still detected, indicating a relatively inefficient photo-degradation process. The previous addition of 50μL of the GQDs synthesis dispersion into the solution irradiated with UV resulted in an initial decrease of the absorption band of thiomersal that ceased up after 50min UV exposure (Fig. 1C). The formation of increased quantities of Hg2+ (latter converted into Hg for CV-AAS detection) was also observed during this period, stabilizing after 50min (Fig. 1D). As the solution of thiomersal in the presence of GQDs was exposed to UV, the opacity of the liquid increased indicating the aggregation of the carbon nanostructures (Fig. 2 ), thus decreasing the catalytic properties of the carbon structures until thiomersal degradation was no longer observed.

The experiment using dispersed GQDs (50 μL of the synthesis dispersion) was repeated by irradiating the solution with visible radiation. In this case, the degradation of thiomersal further continued up to the point that its absorbance spectrum almost reached baseline (Fig. 3A) at 80min. In the graphic inserted (Fig. 3A) it can be seen the thiomersal degradation monitored at two wavelengths (205nm, and 233nm) in function of visible irradiation time (up to 80min). The Hg measured (by CV-AAS) from aliquots of the solution along the experiment also increased up to 60min visible radiation exposure (Fig. 3B), indication that a more effective degradation occurred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the conditions to improve efficiency of the visible radiation photo-degradation of thiomersal mediated by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The study of experimental conditions was made aiming to reduce time to achieve quantitative degradation of thiomersal. First, the experiment was performed using thiomersal solutions (100ngL−1) where different volumes of the GQDs synthesis dispersion (from 5 to 100μL, which corresponds to total carbon values from 1.8 to 35.2mg) were added. The experiment was repeated three times by irradiation of samples during 15min with aliquots of the irradiated samples transferred to the chemical reactor (to perform a final fast reduction of the formed Hg2+ with SnCl2) connected to the CV-AAS system. The result was quite interesting since the measured Hg increased up to 17μL, sharply decreasing to lower measured values as seen in Fig. 4A. Such decreasing of the detected Hg at higher quantities of GQDs can be explained by repeating the experiments with the chemical reactor (still connected to the CV-AAS system) placed inside the photochemical reactor. The experiment indicated that GQDs itself not only converts thiomersal into Hg2+ but also has the ability to convert the formed Hg2+ into elemental Hg. Thus the release of Hg was continuous along the irradiation time (time profile a of Fig. 4B) and increased as the added GQDs dispersion volume were higher than 17μL (time profiles b and c of Fig. 4B), corroborating results shown in Fig. 4A. Despite this fact, the option in this work was to perform a discontinued procedure to form Hg form Hg2+ by using SnCl2 in the chemical reactor rather than try to perform the slower formation of Hg using GQDs. Therefore, the chosen condition was the use of 17μL of synthesis GQDs dispersion (equivalent to 6mg total carbon) that seemed to produce the best efficient formation of Hg2+ with lower further reduction to Hg.

The pH of the irradiated solution was adjusted (by adding small volumes of a 0.1molL−1 HCl solution) and the best pH range for the photo-degradation of thiomersal was between 4.5 and 5.5 (Fig. 5A). At lower pH environment, it is reported that H+ compete with thiomersal to the sites on the surface of the GQDs, reducing its catalytic capability [25]. Alternatively, as the pH values becomes higher than 6.0, the negatively charged GQDs repulses the thiomersal anion, preventing them to make contact.

The light exposure time was studied using thiomersal solutions at pH5.0 containing 17μL of the synthesis dispersion of GQDs. Different test tubes were placed in the reactor and one of them was taken out (every 5min) to select an aliquot to be placed in the reduction reaction cell and measure Hg using CV-AAS. It was found that the maximum Hg signal was achieved after 25min of light exposure (Fig. 5B).

The addition of either formic acid or hydrogen peroxide is reported to improve degradation of thiomersal [18,19]. Therefore, amounts of either of these compounds were added into the visible irradiated solution containing GQDs in order to cover the range from 1 to 12% v/v. The presence of formic acid did not increase the mercury signal measured by CV-AAS. In contrast, the presence of H2O2 at 1 or 2% improved the Hg signal measured by almost 70% (Fig. 5C). The addition of H2O2 also reduced the light exposure time to 20min (Fig. 5D). An additional study was made to evaluate the effect of H2O2 in the photo-degradation of thiomeral but in absence of GQDs. The study demonstrated that only H2O2 is not enough to promote degradation of thiomersal by visible radiation.

A Hg speciation study was made (using GC-CV-AAS) in order to monitor the formation of EtHg+ previous to the formation of Hg2+, under light exposure (mediated by GQDs) at the selected conditions. It was observed (Fig. 6A) that the amounts formed of EtHg+ (at retention time, tR, about 5min) increased from 5 to 15min of visible light exposure time but they are very low (at pg level in 50μL sampled volume), which indicates a fast conversion to Hg2+. After 20min light irradiation, the concentration of EtHg+ drastically reduced, indicating that the photochemical reaction approaches the end. The signal from EtHg+ measured at 0min is due to the degradation of thiomersal, mediated by GQDs, under environmental light. Therefore, photo-reaction already occurs when GQDs is mixed to the thiomersal and solution is not protected from ambient light before placing into the reactor.

The interaction of the photon-excited GQDs with dissolved molecular oxygen, forming the very reactive superoxide radical, and with water, forming peroxide and hydroxyl radicals, produce a proper environment to degrade the nearby thiomersal, besides the possible direct interaction of thiomersal with the photo-excited GQDs, in a possible similar mechanisms proposed by Roushani et al. [25]. Degradation due to the thiomersal self-photosensitization (in the presence of GQDs) pathway is not expected since it does not absorb significantly in the visible range.

These photocatalytic processes degrade thiomersal forming EtHg+ that is then converted into Hg2+, which in turn, in a reducing environment, is slowly reduced to Hg as indicated in Fig. 4B. In the presence of H2O2, the conversion of Hg2+ to Hg becomes more effective due to the presence of a larger quantity of free radicals formed from H2O2 due to the incidence of light and the interaction with GQDs (profile d of Fig. 4B) but not fast enough to allow proper quantification by CV-AAS. That is the reason that a two-step procedure, using fast reduction made in a reduction cell with SnCl2, was chosen for analytical purposes. Besides, the use of SnCl2 as a reducing agent is very selective in reducing Hg2+, not producing foams or bubbles and not reducing organomercury species into Hg.

RP-HPLC with absorption photometric detection was performed in the thiomersal solutions submitted to the photochemical procedure mediated by GQDs (Fig. 6B). The conditions used were the ones that enable resolved peaks from degradation products TSA and DTBA [3]. Before light exposure, a sole peak of thiomersal is observed at tR =6.5min. As sample was exposed to light, the thiomersal peak rapidly disappeared (within 5min) and a group of non-resolved peaks appeared in the 3 to 5min tR range (that include feature characteristic of TSA), which also decrease over time, forming non-absorbing degraded species, after 10min of light exposure in acidic conditions. The appearing of a group of non-resolved peaks and their disappearance over time indicated the formation of a myriad of products, under the photochemical reaction conditions, that are further degraded to more simple non-absorbing species.

In order to evaluate the photo-catalytic effect of a fixed amount of GQDs on thiomersal, an experiment was made by measuring the Hg formed by varying the amount of the analyte from 10ngL−1 up to 500ngL−1. It was found that the magnitude of the measured Hg was directly proportional to the thiomersal content in the whole tested concentration range (Fig. 7A). Thus this defined amount of GQDs is enough to three orders of magnitude of thiomersal.

A kinetic study have shown that a pseudo-first order model holds for the photo-degradation of thiomersal as the GQDs concentration is kept at the fixed value that enabled best catalytic performance. The relationships between the initial concentration of thiomersal (C0) and the concentration of this analyte over time (Ct) in function of irradiation time with visible light, shown in Fig. 7B and Fig. 7C, corroborate such kinetic model. The experiment was made using three different initial concentrations of thiomersal (100, 200 and 300ngL−1) and the obtained pseudo-first model kinetic constants where very close (respectively 0.1103; 0.1159 and 0.1154min−1).

Finally, the effect of the presence of MeHg+ and EtHg+ was evaluated in the photo-degradation of thiomersal mediated by GQDs. It was found that the presence of MeHg+ does not affect the expected Hg signal produced by thiomersal (at 100ngL−1) even when MeHg+ was at the same concentration of thiomersal in the irradiated mixture. It is also important to point out that MeHg+ alone did not produce any signal above the expected baseline Hg signal. The selectivity towards MeHg+ is interesting and should be further studied in a future work. In contrast, the presence of EtHg+ produced an almost equivalent Hg signal that thiomersal produces. This can be observed in Fig. 7D showing the signal ratios achieved by the thiomersal alone and the mixture containing the thiomersal in presence of EtHg+ (at concentrations of 1, 10 and 100ngL−1). In samples containing thiomersal and EtHg+ (as a degradation product) the Hg signal computed is totally related to the original non-degraded thiomersal.

A sequence of standards covering concentrations of thiomersal from 10ngL−1 up to 500ngL−1 were used to construct three analytical curves (each one in a different day). A typical linear model is represented by the eq. Y=(4.90±0.02Lng−1)X+(12.5±4.6) with R2 =0.9984. The limit of detection (6ngL−1) and limit of quantification (20ngL−1) were respectively achieved as the concentrations of thiomersal that enabled the Hg measured signal equivalent to 3 s and 10 s, where s was the standard deviation of the first measurable concentration of the analytical curve. The LOD value achieved in the present work is compared to the ones reported in literature in Table 1.

Precision was evaluated as instrumental precision (1.3%), using the coefficient of variation of the signal produced by the analysis of 10 aliquots of the same thiomersal standard solution (at 100ngL−1). The intermediary precision (2.0%) was evaluated as the coefficient of variation of the analyte recovery achieved by the analysis of ten independent thiomersal standard solutions (at 100ngL−1).

Samples were analyzed to test the proposed method. One was a thiomersal based pharmaceutical formulation (9mgL−1) that was diluted 1000 times before analysis. The others were urine sample and a pharmaceutical industry effluent that were fortified with thiomersal (at, 100, 200 and 300ngL−1). Analyzes of non-fortified urine and pharmaceutical effluent samples showed Hg2+ mercury residues at the concentration of 7 and 11ngL−1 respectively.

In terms of analytical frequency, the photo-reaction could be made in ten different tubes at the same time (20min treatment). Then, while a second load of tubes are inserted and irradiated, the first set of samples can be analyzed. Each solution can be determined in 2min requiring the additional 1min to perform cleaning prior to the next analysis (30min total for the determination step for 10 samples).

Analyses were made in triplicate (in three different days) and the recovered results were 8.3mgL−1 (92.2±0.1%) for the pharmaceutical formulation (already corrected for dilution), For urine the results were 97.2±2ngL−1 (recovery of 97.2±1.0%), 193.4±1.0ngL−1 (recovery of 96.7±1.0%) and 294.5±3 ngL−1 (recovery of 98.1±1.0%). For the pharmaceutical industry effluent samples the results were 95.6±2ngL−1 (recovery of 95.6±1.0%), 188±2.0ngL−1 (recovery of 94.0±1.1%) and 292.8±3 ngL−1 (recovery of 97.6±1.0%).

Cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry, using a multipass cell, provided very sensitive indirect determination of thiomersal, which was degraded in a photochemical reactor (using graphene quantum dots and visible radiation), then rapidly reduced to Hg with SnCl2. Degradation with graphene quantum dots was very effective (following a pseudo-first order kinetic) and made under adjusted conditions to maximize formation of Hg2+ and minimize formation of Hg in the photochemical reaction step. The method was tested in samples with satisfactory recoveries at the ng L−1 range, which is at least one order of magnitude lower than the most sensitive method reported in the literature for thiomersal.

Aucelio thanks scholarships from FAPERJ (E-26/201.406/2014) and CNPq (302888/20132-6). Miranda-Andrades thank the student scholarship from CAPES. Toloza (E-26/200.435/2016) and Romani (E-26/201.750/2015) thank FAPERJ for scholarships. Freire Jr. acknowledges scholarship from CNPq.
